title: AI Coding Assistant Pricing in 2026
subtitle: From flat subscriptions to token budgets, AI credits, cache economics, and real agent-session capacity.

slides.title: AI Coding Pricing in 2026
slides.subtitle: The end of the flat-fee era: compare coding tools by agent-session capacity, not monthly sticker price.
slides.speakerNotes: Open by framing the shift: autocomplete and chat were cheap enough to bundle, but agentic coding reads repos, edits files, runs tests, and retries. Pricing now looks more like cloud computing than a gym membership.

slides.title: Flat-fee pricing broke when coding became agentic
slides.statement: One human prompt can now trigger dozens of model calls, repeated repository context, tool schemas, test logs, and retry loops.
supports: Autocomplete was one cheap inference path.
Agents run multi-step loops that compound context cost.
The useful unit is no longer a message; it is a completed task.
slides.speakerNotes: Use the article's seven-step loop: read files, plan, edit, run tests, parse errors, re-read context, try again. The important shift is cost per outcome, not cost per prompt.

slides.title: A coding request becomes an economic loop

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart LR Prompt[user prompt] --> Loop[agent loop
read - edit - test - retry] Loop --> OpenAI[OpenAI API
direct token meter] Loop --> CodexPlan[ChatGPT Codex
plan windows + credits] Loop --> Copilot[GitHub Copilot
AI Credits bucket] Loop --> Claude[Claude Code
rolling plan windows] OpenAI --> OCost[fresh + cached input
output] CodexPlan --> XCost[message/task limits
then add-on credits] Copilot --> CCost[token cost converted
1 credit = 1 cent] Claude --> ACost[plan credits
session and weekly caps]
diagram.alt: Prompt flows into an agent loop, then into OpenAI, Copilot, Codex plan, and Claude pricing meters.
diagram.caption: Rendered live from the article's first Mermaid diagram.

bullets: The same workflow can be billed as tokens, credits, or rolling windows.
Repeated context and output length dominate long sessions.
Model routing changes the cost curve more than plan price alone.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the core mental model. Every provider puts the guardrail in a different place, but they are all reacting to the same agent loop.

slides.title: Three pricing philosophies are converging

columns.heading: OpenAI / Codex
columns.body: Raw token utility billing or plan windows with add-on credits.
bullets: Best for tool builders
Risk: costs scale directly

columns.heading: GitHub Copilot
columns.body: Subscription plus AI Credits for premium and agentic work.
bullets: Best IDE integration
Risk: budget predictability

columns.heading: Claude Code
columns.body: CLI subscription windows or direct API billing.
bullets: Best terminal workflow
Risk: interrupted sessions

slides.speakerNotes: Emphasize that the products feel different, but the underlying driver is the same: expensive long-context agent work.

slides.title: Translate plans into token-like units
slides.lede: Comparing sticker prices hides the actual unit of work. Normalize everything to agent-call capacity.
bullets: API billing: fresh input + cached input + cache writes + output.
GitHub Copilot: API-equivalent dollars converted into AI Credits.
Claude subscriptions: rolling internal credit-like windows.
A full task may use 10, 25, or 50+ model calls.
Reference call: 100K cached + 10K fresh + 2K output tokens.

source.section: How to translate a plan into tokens

slides.speakerNotes: This slide sets up the math. Avoid debating exact future rates; the method remains useful even when price sheets move.

slides.title: A single agent call has four cost surfaces

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TB T[one agent call] --> FI[fresh input tokens] T --> CI[cached input tokens] T --> CW[cache write tokens] T --> OUT[output tokens] FI --> R[provider rate card] CI --> R CW --> R OUT --> R R --> USD[API-equivalent dollars] USD --> GH[GitHub Copilot
multiply by 100
AI Credits] USD --> API[OpenAI or Anthropic API
bill directly] USD --> ClaudePlan[Claude subscription
compare with inferred
plan credit bucket]
diagram.alt: Fresh input, cached input, cache write, and output tokens flow through provider rate cards into dollars or credits.
diagram.caption: Live render of the article's token accounting diagram.

bullets: Cached context is cheaper, but not always free.
Output is often the expensive side of the bill.
Credits are a wrapper over provider-specific token economics.
slides.speakerNotes: Point out that caching discipline is a product and prompt design issue. Stable prefixes and compact context are now economic controls.

slides.title: Model routing is the OpenAI cost model
slides.caption: Reference call: 100K cached input + 10K fresh input + 2K output.

source.section: OpenAI: utility pricing and model routing

slides.speakerNotes: The point is not that one model always wins. The spread is large enough that routing policy matters more than the headline subscription.

slides.title: Codex through ChatGPT is plan-window economics
slides.lede: Included usage and purchased credits are not the same economic product.
bullets: Plus and Business expose five-hour local/cloud-task windows.
The public docs publish ranges, not stable monthly dollar quotas.
Purchased credits use a token-based Codex rate card.
Community dollar-equivalent conversions are useful but unofficial.
Fast mode improves latency but burns credits faster.
slides.speakerNotes: Be careful here: distinguish validated plan shapes from inferred dollar-equivalent values. The slide intentionally avoids over-claiming.

slides.title: AI Credits turn Copilot into a shared budget
slides.caption: Using GPT-5.3-Codex reference-call math from the article.
slides.speakerNotes: Enterprise pooling helps because usage is uneven. But automated reviews, background agents, and CI workflows need guardrails.

slides.title: Claude Code trades wallet risk for window risk

columns.heading: Subscription
bullets: Good monthly value
Can interrupt refactors

columns.heading: API billing
bullets: Better automation path
Costs spike in long loops

columns.heading: Team / Enterprise
bullets: Useful governance
Higher baseline seat cost

slides.speakerNotes: This is a workflow choice. Claude Code is strong for terminal-native engineering, but you must manage context and session boundaries.

slides.title: Claude's caching surprise changes repeated-context math

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart LR Context[100K repeated context] --> Cold[cold turn
cache write] Context --> Warm[warm turn
cache read] Cold --> SubCold[Claude plan
100K input counts] Cold --> ApiCold[API
100K write at 1.25x] Warm --> SubWarm[Claude plan
cache read = 0 credits
only new input + output] Warm --> ApiWarm[API or Copilot meter
100K cache read at 10%] SubWarm --> Value[subscription value expands
in repeated agent loops] ApiWarm --> MeterAnxiety[long loops still draw down
credits or dollars]
diagram.alt: Cold cache counts a large context write, while warm cache counts mainly new input and output for subscription usage.
diagram.caption: Live render of the cold-cache and warm-cache examples.

bullets: Cold turns pay for cache writes.
Warm subscription turns may avoid charging cache reads.
API-style meters still price repeated context reads.
slides.speakerNotes: Use this slide to explain why Claude subscriptions can feel dramatically cheaper for repeated-context sessions than API-style metering. Mark the underlying subscription figures as inferred, not official.

slides.title: The cheapest model can be the expensive choice
slides.statement: A cheap failed attempt can cost more than a strong first pass once retries, review time, and human cleanup enter the bill.
supports: More iterations resend context and tool schemas.
Subtle code errors move cost into review and CI.
Senior-engineer cleanup dominates small token savings.

source.section: When the cheap model is the expensive choice

slides.speakerNotes: The article's example is Sonnet at $0.30 vs Haiku at $0.18 plus 20 minutes of human cleanup. Token bill alone is misleading.

slides.title: Route by verification cost, not habit

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart LR Task[coding task] --> Verifiable{cheap to verify
and cheap to retry?} Verifiable -->|yes| Small[start with small model] Verifiable -->|no| Strong[start with strong model] Small --> Pass{passed in 1-2 tries?} Pass -->|yes| Done[ship - cheapest path] Pass -->|no| Escalate[escalate to stronger model
log the escalation] Escalate --> Strong Strong --> Review[human review] Review --> Done
diagram.alt: Task verification decision routes easy work to small models and hard or risky work to stronger models.
diagram.caption: Live render of the article's model-routing loop.

bullets: Start cheap only when verification is cheap.
Escalate after one or two failed attempts.
Log escalation reasons to tune policy.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the practical rule: minimum cost to a correct outcome, not minimum cost per call.

slides.title: Five controls keep agentic coding costs sane
items: Make prompts cache-friendly with stable prefixes.
Prune context aggressively before each retry.
Route models by task risk and verification cost.
Put budgets, alerts, and kill switches near the workflow.
slides.speakerNotes: Close the main talk here if time is short. The appendix can carry exact numbers; the durable message is operational discipline.

slides.title: Make cost visible without shaming developers
slides.lede: The goal is feedback loops, not guilt. Put cost signals where engineers already work.
bullets: Track per-user, per-team, model-level, and CI-linked usage.
Use soft warnings before hard limits.
Attribute background agents and automated code review separately.
Create a named reason for every model escalation.
Review failed cheap attempts, not just expensive successful ones.
slides.speakerNotes: This expands the playbook for engineering leaders. The cultural framing matters: visibility should improve routing decisions, not punish experimentation.

slides.title: AI coding is now priced like cloud computing
items: Choose plans by real agent-session capacity.
Cache static context and avoid file dumps.
Use the cheapest model whose mistakes are affordable.
Measure human cleanup as part of total cost.
Treat prompt and workflow design as FinOps controls.
slides.speakerNotes: Final line from the article: AI coding used to be priced like a gym membership; now it is priced like cloud computing.
